pattern_recognition: Array Manipulation, In-place Modification; the problem requires rearranging elements without using extra space, which signals the need for efficient traversal and swapping techniques.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Iterate through the array, collect non-zero elements, and then append zeros to the end.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Create a new array to hold non-zero elements. 2. Traverse the original array and add non-zero elements to the new array. 3. Fill the remaining positions in the new array with zeros. 4. Copy the new array back to the original.
approaches.code_python:
from typing import List

def moveZeroes(nums: List[int]) -> None:
    non_zero = [num for num in nums if num != 0]
    zero_count = len(nums) - len(non_zero)
    nums[:] = non_zero + [0] * zero_count

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use two pointers to rearrange the elements in-place, minimizing space usage and maintaining order.
approaches.algorithm: 1. Initialize a pointer for the position of the last non-zero element. 2. Traverse the array; whenever a non-zero is found, swap it with the element at the last non-zero position pointer. 3. Increment the pointer after each swap. 4. Continue until the end of the array.

dry_run: For the input [0, 1, 0, 3, 12]: Start with a pointer at the beginning. First, encounter 0 (skip), then 1 (swap with 0), array becomes [1, 0, 0, 3, 12]. Next, 0 (skip), then 3 (swap with 0), array becomes [1, 3, 0, 0, 12]. Finally, 12 (swap with 0), resulting in [1, 3, 12, 0, 0].
edge_cases: Empty input results in no action. A single element array remains unchanged. Duplicates like [0, 0, 0] yield no change. Large inputs should be handled efficiently without exceeding memory limits.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to maintain the order of non-zero elements can lead to incorrect outputs. 2. Using extra space unnecessarily when an in-place solution is required. 3. Not handling edge cases like all zeros or a single non-zero element properly.
